perturbation.original_text: I agree that Motorola, and its officers, agents, and employees are not liable for injuries or property damage resulting from my attendance at the event. I agree to release and hold all of the above named persons and entities harmless from any claim for injury, damage or loss of any kind.
perturbation.changed_text: I agree that Motorola, and its officers, agents, and employees are not liable for injuries or property damage resulting from my attendance at the event, regardless of Motorola's negligence or willful misconduct. I agree to release and hold all of the above named persons and entities harmless from any claim for injury, damage or loss of any kind, even if caused by Motorola's own actions.
perturbation.explanation: This modification attempts to release Motorola from liability even for their own negligence or willful misconduct. This contradicts the principle that parties cannot contract away liability for gross negligence or intentional torts, depending on jurisdiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General common law principle against exculpation for gross negligence or intentional misconduct; potentially against public policy.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the principle that parties cannot contract away liability for gross negligence or intentional torts. Many jurisdictions find such waivers unenforceable as against public policy. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 195 addresses unenforceable exculpatory clauses.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: exculpatory clause | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you exculpatory clause An exculpatory clause is part of a contract that prevents one party from holding the other party liable for damages related to the contract. Exculpatory clauses are used quite often in purchases such as the ones included with an amusement park or plane ticket. Courts often look down on exculpatory clauses because they allow a party to skirt responsibility, and courts can strike down exculpatory clauses when they are hidden in a contract or have too broad of coverage that violates public policy. [Last reviewed in July of 2021 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE LIFE EVENTS accidents & injuries (tort law) mass tort litigation product liability law standards of tort liability tort damages contracts wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text attempts to release Motorola from liability even for their own negligence or willful misconduct. This directly contradicts the principle that parties cannot contract away liability for gross negligence or intentional torts, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. The scraped snippet from Wex defines an exculpatory clause as "part of a contract that prevents one party from holding the other party liable for damages related to the contract." While exculpatory clauses are sometimes used, the snippet states that "courts can strike down exculpatory clauses when they are hidden in a contract or have too broad of coverage that violates public policy." In this case, attempting to waive liability for one's own negligence or willful misconduct is likely to be considered against public policy, leading to a contradiction as the changed text is unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Any violation of the foregoing will result in your visit being terminated, and possibly legal action.
perturbation.changed_text: Any violation of the foregoing will result in your visit being terminated, and immediate forfeiture of any rights you may have against Motorola, regardless of the nature or value of those rights.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a penalty that is disproportionate to the violation, potentially violating principles of contract law regarding unconscionable or punitive damages.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General contract law principle against unconscionable penalties or liquidated damages.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification introduces a disproportionate penalty for violating the NDA. Contract law generally disallows penalty clauses that are not reasonably related to actual damages, and it is a penalty that is likely to be unconscionable, or violate public policy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-718. Liquidation or Limitation of Damages; Deposits. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-718. Liquidation or Limitation of Damages; Deposits. (1) Damages for breach by either party may be liquidated in the agreement but only at an amount which is reasonable in the light of the anticipated or actual harm caused by the breach, the difficulties of proof of loss, and the inconvenience or nonfeasibility of otherwise obtaining an adequate remedy. A term fixing unreasonably large liquidated damages is void as a penalty. (2) Where the seller justifiably withholds delivery of goods because of the buyer's breach, the buyer is entitled to restitution of any amount by which the sum of his payments exceeds (a) the amount to which the seller is entitled by virtue of terms liquidating the seller's damages in accordance with subsection (1), or (b) in the absence of such terms, twenty per cent of the value of the total performance for which the buyer is obligated under the contract or $500, whichever is smaller. (3) The buyer's right to restitution under subsection (2) is subject to offset to the extent that the seller establishes (a) a right to recover damages under the provisions of this Article other than subsection (1), and (b) the amount or value of any benefits received by the buyer directly or indirectly by reason of the contract . (4) Where a seller has received payment in goods their reasonable value or the proceeds of their resale shall be treated as payments for the purposes of subsection (2); but if the seller has notice of the buyer's breach before reselling goods received in part performance, his resale is subject to the conditions laid down in this Article on resale by an aggrieved seller (Section 2-706 ). Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a penalty (immediate forfeiture of rights) that is disproportionate to a potential violation of the original contract, which involves an NDA. This contradicts the principle that damages must be reasonable in relation to the actual harm caused by the breach. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "A term fixing unreasonably large liquidated damages is void as a penalty." The change makes the contract likely unenforceable because the penalty is not tied to actual damages and is therefore punitive, not compensatory.

perturbation.original_text: I will keep everything I see, touch or hear strictly confidential. In particular, I agree NOT, to blog or otherwise post any Confidential Motorola Information to any social media websites.
perturbation.changed_text: I will keep everything I see, touch or hear strictly confidential, in perpetuity, without any exceptions for generally known or publicly available information. In particular, I agree NOT, to blog or otherwise post any information, regardless of its source or availability, to any social media websites.
perturbation.explanation: This change attempts to prevent disclosure of information even if it's already public knowledge, which is an unreasonably broad restriction on speech and potentially violates the First Amendment (especially if applied broadly).
perturbation.contradicted_law: First Amendment to the United States Constitution; overbroad restrictions on speech.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification broadens the confidentiality obligations to cover even publicly available information, which conflicts with the First Amendment's protection of free speech. Courts are unlikely to enforce restrictions on disclosing information that is already in the public domain.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: First Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you First Amendment The First Amendment guarantees freedoms concerning religion, expression, assembly, and the right to petition. It forbids Congress from both promoting one religion over others and also restricting an individual’s religious practices . It guarantees freedom of expression by prohibiting Congress from restricting the press or the rights of individuals to speak freely. It also guarantees the right of citizens to assemble peaceably and to petition their government . Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment I Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion, or prohibiting the free exercise thereof; or abridging the freedom of speech, or of the press; or the right of the people peaceably to assemble, and to petition the government for a redress of grievances. perturbation.justification: The changed text expands the confidentiality obligations to include publicly available information, which conflicts with the First Amendment's protection of free speech. The original text only restricted confidential Motorola information. The modified text removes this qualifier, attempting to prohibit disclosure of any information regardless of its source or availability. This directly contradicts the First Amendment, which guarantees freedom of speech. The scraped snippet 2 states "Congress shall make no law... abridging the freedom of speech, or of the press". The modification in the contract is an abridgment of free speech because it attempts to restrict speech about even public knowledge.

perturbation.original_text: I will NOT take photographs or recordings.
perturbation.changed_text: I will NOT take photographs or recordings, even for personal use or to document safety hazards.
perturbation.explanation: This change prohibits taking photographs or recordings even to document potential safety hazards, which could violate OSHA regulations requiring employers to allow employees to report unsafe working conditions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 29 U.S. Code § 662 - Inspections, investigations, and recordkeeping
perturbation.law_explanation: This change restricts the ability to take photos of safety hazards. The restriction goes against federal law. 29 U.S.C. § 662, relating to OSHA, allows inspections and investigations, which can reasonably be interpreted to include the right to document safety hazards, especially if an employer does not provide a mechanism to do so.

Injunction proceedings (a) Petition by Secretary to restrain imminent dangers; scope of order The United States district courts shall have jurisdiction, upon petition of the Secretary, to restrain any conditions or practices in any place of employment which are such that a danger exists which could reasonably be expected to cause death or serious physical harm immediately or before the imminence of such danger can be eliminated through the enforcement procedures otherwise provided by this ch
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Inspections, Investigations, and Recordkeeping | Occupational Safety and Health Administration Skip to main content SEC. 8. Inspections, Investigations, and Recordkeeping (a) More... 29 USC 657 In order to carry out the purposes of this Act, the Secretary, upon presenting appropriate credentials to the owner, operator, or agent in charge, is authorized -- (1) to enter without delay and at reasonable times any factory, plant, establishment, construction site, or other area, workplace or environment where work is performed by an employee of an employer; and (2) to inspect and investigate during regular working hours and at other reasonable times, and within reasonable limits and in a reasonable manner, any such place of employment and all pertinent conditions, structures, machines, apparatus, devices, equipment, and materials therein, and to question privately any such employer, owner, operator, agent or employee. (b) In making his inspections and investigations under this Act the Secretary may require the attendance and testimony of witnesses and the production of evidence under oath. Witnesses shall be paid the same fees and mileage that are paid witnesses in the courts of the United States. In case of a contumacy, failure, or refusal of any person to obey such an order, any district court of the United States or the United States courts of any territory or possession, within the jurisdiction of which such person is found, or resides or transacts business, upon the application by the Secretary, shall have jurisdiction to issue to such person an order requiring such person to appear to produce evidence if, as, and when so ordered, and to give testimony relating to the matter under investigation or in question, and any failure to obey such order of the court may be punished by said court as a contempt thereof. (c) (1) Each employer shall make, keep and preserve, and make available to the Secretary or the Secretary of Health and Human Services, such records regarding his activities relating to this Act as the Secretary, in cooperation with the Secretary of Health and Human Services, may prescribe by regulation as necessary or appropriate for the enfo
perturbation.justification: The modified text in the contract prohibits taking photographs or recordings, even to document potential safety hazards. This directly contradicts the spirit and intent of OSHA regulations, which, as the Law Explanation suggests, can reasonably be interpreted to include the right to document safety hazards, especially if an employer does not provide a mechanism to do so. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 29 USC 657, authorizes the Secretary to 'inspect and investigate' workplaces and 'all pertinent conditions.' While it doesn't explicitly mention employee rights to document, preventing employees from documenting hazards significantly impedes the process of identifying and rectifying unsafe conditions, undermining the purpose of OSHA regulations to ensure workplace safety.

perturbation.original_text: I understand my Motorola host must be a Motorola employee or Motorola Contractor with Escort Privileges, and that my Motorola host and myself are accountable for my actions.
perturbation.changed_text: I understand my Motorola host must be a Motorola employee or Motorola Contractor with Escort Privileges, and that my Motorola host and myself are fully and solely liable for any incidents that occur, regardless of Motorola's contribution or negligence.
perturbation.explanation: This attempts to shift full liability to the visitor and host, even if Motorola's negligence contributes to an incident. This is likely unenforceable and against public policy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of negligence and comparative fault.
perturbation.law_citation: Restatement (Third) of Torts: Apportionment of Liability § 8
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification makes the visitor fully liable, ignoring negligence on Motorola's end. According to The Restatement (Third) of Torts: Apportionment of Liability § 8, responsibility is divided based on percentages.

perturbation.justification: The modified text attempts to shift full liability to the visitor and host, even if Motorola's negligence contributes to an incident. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both state that this contradicts established tort law principles, which apportion liability based on negligence. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the core principle of apportioning liability is well-established, and the changed text directly contradicts this by assigning full liability regardless of Motorola's negligence, making the clause likely unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: I agree that my admittance into the Motorola facility is a revocable license, and is subject to revocation. I agree that I may be removed from the Motorola Facility at Motorola’s discretion.
perturbation.changed_text: I agree that my admittance into the Motorola facility is a revocable license, and is subject to immediate revocation for any reason, including discriminatory reasons prohibited by law. I agree that I may be removed from the Motorola Facility at Motorola’s sole discretion, without any recourse or explanation.
perturbation.explanation: This clause allows Motorola to revoke access for discriminatory reasons, which would violate anti-discrimination laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Title II of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, prohibiting discrimination in places of public accommodation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Title II of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 prohibits discrimination based on race, color, religion, or national origin in places of public accommodation. While a Motorola facility may not be a traditional place of public accommodation, broadly stating 'any reason, including discriminatory reasons prohibited by law' for revoking admittance violates the spirit and potentially the letter of anti-discrimination laws if the facility is open to the public, even for events.

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly allows for revocation of admittance for discriminatory reasons, which directly contradicts the principles of civil rights as outlined in 42 USC Ch. 21, which covers civil rights and equal rights under the law. While the snippet does not provide specific wording against discrimination in admittance to facilities, the broad scope of civil rights legislation suggests that allowing discrimination in this context undermines the spirit and potentially the letter of the law. The original text provided no such allowance for discrimination. The modification introduces a clause that is likely to fail legal scrutiny due to its explicit allowance of discriminatory practices, making it a direct and serious contradiction.
